---
title: "Changing residual coupling with rho12"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Changing residual coupling with rho12}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

This article introduces the first flagship `drmTMB` model: bivariate
location-scale regression with predictor-dependent residual correlation. The
scientific idea is that two responses can change in mean, change in residual
variance, and change in residual coupling.

Current fixed-effect syntax:

```r
drmTMB(
  drm_formula(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2,
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1,
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

The central modelling question is whether predictors change residual coupling
between two responses, not only their means or variances.

This is why `rho12` is the selling point of `drmTMB`. A location-scale model
asks whether predictors change residual SDs. A location-coscale model asks
whether predictors also change the residual covariance structure between two
responses; in the current bivariate Gaussian model, that structure is the
residual correlation `rho12`. In ecology and evolution, this can ask whether
the residual association between traits such as body mass and litter size
differs among terrestrial, aquatic, and aerial lifestyles after modelling
response-specific means and dispersion.

## Equations for the implemented model

For observation `i`, the implemented fixed-effect bivariate Gaussian model is:

```text
[y1_i, y2_i]' | mu1_i, mu2_i, sigma1_i, sigma2_i, rho12_i
  ~ MVN([mu1_i, mu2_i]', Omega_i)

mu1_i = X_mu1[i, ] beta_mu1
mu2_i = X_mu2[i, ] beta_mu2
log(sigma1_i) = X_sigma1[i, ] beta_sigma1
log(sigma2_i) = X_sigma2[i, ] beta_sigma2
eta_rho12_i = X_rho12[i, ] beta_rho12
rho12_i = 0.99999999 * tanh(eta_rho12_i)
```

The observation-specific residual covariance matrix is:

```text
Omega_i[1, 1] = sigma1_i^2
Omega_i[2, 2] = sigma2_i^2
Omega_i[1, 2] = Omega_i[2, 1] = rho12_i * sigma1_i * sigma2_i
```

Equivalently:

```text
sigma1_i = exp(X_sigma1[i, ] beta_sigma1)
sigma2_i = exp(X_sigma2[i, ] beta_sigma2)
eta_rho12_i = X_rho12[i, ] beta_rho12
rho12_i = 0.99999999 * tanh(eta_rho12_i)
```

The matching `drmTMB` syntax is:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2,
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1,
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

The mapping is:

| Symbol | Meaning | R syntax source |
|---|---|---|
| `y1_i`, `y2_i` | observed responses | left-hand sides of `mu1` and `mu2` formulas |
| `X_mu1`, `X_mu2` | response-specific mean design matrices | right-hand sides of `mu1` and `mu2` |
| `mu1_i`, `mu2_i` | fitted response-specific means | columns of `fitted(fit)` |
| `X_sigma1`, `X_sigma2` | response-specific residual scale design matrices | right-hand sides of `sigma1` and `sigma2` |
| `X_rho12` | residual correlation design matrix | right-hand side of `rho12` |
| `sigma1_i`, `sigma2_i` | residual SDs for responses 1 and 2 | `predict(fit, dpar = "sigma1")`, `predict(fit, dpar = "sigma2")` |
| `rho12_i` | residual response-response correlation | `rho12(fit)` |

The fixed effects may be the same, overlapping, or different across
parameters. For example, `x1` can appear in all five formulae, while `x2` might
appear only in `mu1`, `sigma1`, and `rho12`. Each parameter still gets its own
coefficient vector.

If both responses have the same location predictors, `mvbind()` is a shorthand
for the two location formulas:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    mvbind(y1, y2) ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

This expands internally to `mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2` and
`mu2 = y2 ~ x1 + x2`. Use explicit `mu1` and `mu2` formulas whenever the
responses need different location predictors.

## Future group-level equations

The future double-hierarchical version should also allow group-level random
intercepts and random slopes in the mean structure, following the
individual-differences framework of O'Dea et al. (2022):

```r
drmTMB(
  formula = drm_formula(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2 + (1 + x2 | p | ID),
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1      + (1 + x2 | p | ID),
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x1 + x2
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)
```

The two terms in `(1 + x2 | p | ID)` create the personality and plasticity
random effects in the mean part. `sigma1` and `sigma2` are the residual scale
parameters; additional random-effect scale components should be named and
extracted as group-level standard deviations, not confused with residual
`sigma`.

Symbolically, the mean part would look like:

```text
mu1_ij = X_mu1[ij, ] beta_mu1 + b_0_1j + b_x2_1j * x2_ij
mu2_ij = X_mu2[ij, ] beta_mu2 + b_0_2j + b_x2_2j * x2_ij

[b_0_1j, b_x2_1j, b_0_2j, b_x2_2j]' ~ MVN(0, Sigma_mu_ID)
```

The covariance matrix `Sigma_mu_ID` contains group-level standard deviations
and group-level correlations. Those correlations answer questions about
between-group association in intercepts and slopes. They are not `rho12_i`.
The parameter `rho12_i` remains the observation-level residual correlation in
`Omega_i`.

Minimal runnable example:

```r
set.seed(1)
n <- 300
dat <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n))
mu1 <- 0.2 + 0.5 * dat$x1 + 0.2 * dat$x2
mu2 <- -0.1 + 0.4 * dat$x1
sigma1 <- exp(-0.2 + 0.2 * dat$x1 - 0.1 * dat$x2)
sigma2 <- exp(0.1 - 0.2 * dat$x1)
eta_rho12 <- -0.1 + 0.4 * dat$x3
rho12 <- 0.99999999 * tanh(eta_rho12)
e1 <- rnorm(n)
e2 <- rho12 * e1 + sqrt(1 - rho12^2) * rnorm(n)
dat$y1 <- mu1 + sigma1 * e1
dat$y2 <- mu2 + sigma2 * e2

fit <- drmTMB(
  bf(
    mu1 = y1 ~ x1 + x2,
    mu2 = y2 ~ x1,
    sigma1 = ~ x1 + x2,
    sigma2 = ~ x1,
    rho12 = ~ x3
  ),
  family = c(gaussian(), gaussian()),
  data = dat
)

check_drm(fit)
coef(fit, "rho12")
rho12(fit)[1:5]
rho12(fit, type = "link")[1:5]
```

The `rho12` coefficients are fitted on the linear-predictor scale
`eta_rho12`; the response transform is
`rho12 = 0.99999999 * tanh(eta_rho12)`. Use `rho12(fit)` to read them as
residual correlations. In this example, a positive coefficient for `x3` means
that observations with larger `x3` have stronger positive residual coupling
between `y1` and `y2`, after the model has already accounted for the
predictors in `mu1`, `mu2`, `sigma1`, and `sigma2`.

For ecological and evolutionary examples, `rho12` can represent changing
residual coupling between traits such as activity and boldness, body size and
fecundity, or leaf area and seed number.

The middle `p` in `(1 | p | id)` follows grouped covariance-block syntax for
correlated group-level effects. Labelled one-slope blocks are implemented for
univariate Gaussian `mu`; shared bivariate `mu1`/`mu2` covariance blocks remain
future work. This syntax is distinct from residual `rho12`.

In phylogenetic location-scale models, correlations among phylogenetic mean and
scale effects answer different questions: mean-mean coevolution, scale-scale
co-divergence, and mean-scale evolutionary constraints. Those are group-level
covariance summaries. Residual `rho12` is the observation-level trait coupling
after those structured effects have been accounted for.

The long-term package should therefore support several correlation levels, not
only residual correlation. For example, a comparative model could estimate a
phylogenetic correlation between response-specific evolutionary deviations, a
non-phylogenetic species correlation after shared ancestry is accounted for,
and a residual `rho12` within observations. Those quantities should be named
and extracted separately, for example as `corpars$phylo`,
`corpars$species`, and `rho12(fit)`.
